channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Philosophy of Endeavor
item.description:
Three philosophical traditions converge on the same structure: endeavor is the attempt raised to sustained commitment.

¶Chisholm: endeavoring as the form of intentional action

Chisholm’s analysis (Person and Object, 1976): endeavoring is the general form of intentional activity as such. Every intentional act is an endeavoring, but not every endeavoring is a doing. The distinction: endeavoring can fail. The agent endeavors to move their arm and the arm doesn’t move (paralysis without the agent’s knowledge). The endeavoring occurred; the action (arm-moving) did not.

item.title: The Settlement Path
item.description: The settlement path is the organizing principle of a settlement-carrier system. Every agent, every hook, every skill, every design decision serves a single movement: the transformation of uncertain work into deterministic execution. This is not a metaphor applied after the fact. The system was built around this progression, and understanding the settlement path is understanding why every part of the system exists and where it sits relative to every other part.

item.title: The Temporality of Endeavors
item.description:
An endeavor’s temporal character — whether it is aim-terminated, indefinite, or aim-open — is not a property you choose for flavor. It is a structural fact that determines which operational patterns the endeavor requires. Different temporal structures require different infrastructures. You cannot run an indefinite endeavor with the governance of an aim-terminated one. The shape of your operations loop is not optional; it follows from what kind of striving you are committed to.

¶Aim-terminated endeavors: natural horizons, finite planning

An aim-terminated endeavor has a recognizable end-state. Build a bridge. Complete a novel. Cure a disease. When you reach the aim, the endeavor is done. This temporal shape determines everything about operations.

item.title: Cross-Cutting Concepts: Events, State Machines, Flow, Cadence, Records
item.description:
Some concepts don’t belong to one stage of the operations loop — they span all six. Events, state machines, flow, cadence, and records are the connective tissue that makes the loop one integrated system rather than six disconnected tools.

¶Events as ground truth

Event sourcing (Young 2010) inverts the traditional data model. Instead of storing the current state and modifying it in place, you store the sequence of events that produced the current state. The current state is a derived view — a fold over the event log.

item.title: Dispatch and Delegation: How Work Reaches the Agent Who Does It
item.description:
Dispatch is the act of routing work to the agent who will do it. It is the bridge between planning (work exists and is structured) and execution (work is being done). How this bridge works determines whether the right work reaches the right agent at the right time with the right authority.

¶Coordination mechanisms: Mintzberg

Henry Mintzberg’s The Structuring of Organizations (1979) identifies five mechanisms by which organizations coordinate work. These are not alternatives — most organizations use all five, with different mechanisms dominating at different scales.

item.title: Execution and Monitoring: How Work Gets Done and What Can Go Wrong
item.description:
Execution is the sequence of acts that advances a work entity from “assigned” toward “completed.” It is where plans meet reality — where assumptions are tested, errors occur, and the system’s resilience is measured.

¶State machines and statecharts

Execution is state transition. A task starts in one state and, through a series of acts, reaches another. The execution semantics of the operations loop are defined by which transitions are legal from each state.

item.title: Feedback and Organizational Learning: How Review Findings Become Real Changes
item.description:
Feedback is the act of modifying the system based on what was learned. Without feedback, review is theater — findings are documented and ignored. With feedback, each cycle of the operations loop produces a better-calibrated system. This is the stage that makes the loop a spiral of improvement rather than a circle of repetition.

¶Cybernetic foundations: Wiener and Ashby

Norbert Wiener’s Cybernetics (1948) establishes the mathematical framework for feedback systems. A system under feedback control has: a sensor (measures current state), a comparator (compares measured state to desired state), and an effector (acts to reduce the difference). The loop runs continuously: sense, compare, act, sense again.

item.title: Formal Planning Theory: From STRIPS to Partial-Order Plans
item.description:
A plan is a structured commitment to intended actions ordered by dependencies. This text develops the formal side: how AI planning theory makes the structure of plans mathematically precise, what that precision buys, and where the formalism connects to philosophical accounts and to the repo’s own relational framework.

¶The formal definition

A plan is a pair (O,≺)(\mathcal{O}, \prec):

(O:FinSet(Operation),  ≺⊆O×O:StrictPartialOrder)(\mathcal{O} : \mathrm{FinSet}(\mathrm{Operation}),\; \prec \subseteq \mathcal{O} \times \mathcal{O} : \mathrm{StrictPartialOrder})

together with:

item.title: Personal Task Management: From Eisenhower's Quadrants to Empirical Evidence
item.description: Personal task management is the practice of an individual deciding what to work on, when to work on it, and how to track whether it got done. This survey covers the major practitioner systems — Getting Things Done, time blocking, the Eisenhower matrix, Autofocus, and the Bullet Journal — alongside the academic evidence for whether personal task management actually improves outcomes. The answer is more complicated than the productivity industry suggests.

item.title: Planning and Scheduling: From Critical Path to Seven Generations
item.description:
Planning is the act of creating a structured container of work entities whose collective completion advances a goal. This survey covers how the practice of planning has been understood across operations research, management science, cognitive philosophy, and indigenous governance — because effective organizational planning draws on all of these, not any one of them.

¶Network scheduling: CPM and PERT

The modern discipline of project scheduling begins with two methods published in 1959: the Critical Path Method (Kelley and Walker 1959) and the Program Evaluation and Review Technique (Malcolm et al. 1959).

item.title: Review and Retrospective: How You Assess What Happened vs. What Was Planned
item.description:
Review is the act of comparing actual outcomes against planned outcomes. It takes what happened and what was supposed to happen and produces a judgment: what went right, what went wrong, and why. Review is where the operations loop learns.

¶After Action Review: the Army method

The US Army’s After Action Review (AAR) protocol (Training Circular 25-20, 1993) is the most influential structured review format in the world. Developed from Vietnam-era training reforms and formalized at the National Training Center, the AAR asks four questions:

item.title: The Algebraic Structure of Work: Free Algebras, Ground Terms, and Operational Signatures
item.description:
The work taxonomy distinguishes operations, work, and work units by their degree of specification. This text shows that the same distinction has a precise formalization in universal algebra: operations are signatures, work is term application, work units are ground terms, and the settlement of open work into determined work is the closure of free variables by substitution.

¶The operational signature

Given a signature Σ\Sigma — a set of operation symbols, each with an arity — the work domain defines the operations available. An operation symbol ω∈Σ\omega \in \Sigma with arity nn is the abstract type: “review” is a binary operation (takes a reviewer and a document), “write” is a binary operation (takes a writer and a topic), “dispatch” is a ternary operation (takes a task, an agent, and a capability check).

item.title: The Formal Structure of Delegation: Presheaves, Heyting Algebras, and Roman Legal Doctrine
item.description:
Delegation is the transfer of bounded authority from a principal to a beneficiary, where accountability remains with the principal. This text develops the formal side: a presheaf model where authority is a Heyting algebra, delegation is a restriction map, and the structural facts of delegation (nemo dat, accountability non-transfer, sub-delegation bounds) follow from the algebra.

The model itself — treating authority as a Heyting algebra and delegation as a restriction map — is a proposed interpretation grounded in the repo’s relational framework. The question is whether it is the right model: whether the structural predictions it makes correspond to how delegation actually works in organizational practice.

item.title: The Office as Normative Presheaf: Fiber Doctrine, Roman Law, and Constitutional Opposition
item.description:
An office is a normative position that persists independently of any particular holder. This text develops the formal model: the office as a presheaf of fiber Heyting algebras with nuclear doctrine, the correspondence with Roman legal categories (munus, honor, dignitas), Kantorowicz’s body politic as the presheaf structure, and Madison’s separation of powers as nuclear insulation between offices.

¶Formal tuple

An Office O is a quadruple (H,A,DO,I+)(H, A, D_O, I^+):

item.title: The Ontology of Work Operations
item.description:
Work operations are the foundational ontology applied to work. This is not a metaphor. The base concepts — thing, quality, state, act, container, vessel — map directly onto work entities. A task is a thing. A plan is a container. Status is state. An assignment is an act. The six-stage operations loop (planning, dispatch, execution, tracking, review, feedback) is what happens when you take these primitives seriously as the architecture for organizational coordination.

¶The mapping

¶Things that work operates on

A task is a thing — it can be distinguished from everything else, it has qualities, it changes state. A task has a goal (quality), a status (state), a work type (quality), a priority (quality), an assignee (relation). You can point at a task, name it, track it, complete it. It is not a process or a plan — it is a concrete unit of work with a lifecycle.

item.title: The Settlement of Work Management: How Infrastructure Turns Inquiry Into Process
item.description: As a work system matures, the question “what should I do next?” changes character. In a new project with no infrastructure, answering that question is genuine inquiry — both the meaning of “next” and the method for finding it are open. With some structure in place (a backlog, some priorities), it becomes derivation — the meaning is settled but the answer follows from judgment. With a dependency graph and status tracking, it becomes procedure — follow the rules, but execution involves branching. With a complete dependency graph, WIP limits, and readiness checks, it becomes process — the answer is deterministically computable.

item.title: Tracking and Measurement: How You Know What State Work Is In
item.description:
Tracking is the continuous observation of work entity states. It does not act — it observes and reports. The tracking stage reads the operational state, computes derived metrics, surfaces anomalies, and feeds the review stage with data for comparison against plans.

¶Earned Value Management

Earned Value Management (Fleming and Koppelman 2010) is the standard costing methodology for project tracking. It answers three questions simultaneously: how much work was planned? How much was done? How much did it cost?

item.title: Work Type Classification: Is the 2×2 Enough?
item.description:
The taxonomy classifies work into four types based on two dimensions: whether meaning is settled (do you know what to do?) and whether execution is settled (do you know how to do it?). The result is a 2×2:

Execution settled Execution open
Meaning settled Process Procedure
Meaning open Derivation Inquiry

The question this survey addresses: is this taxonomy adequate? Do established frameworks for classifying work agree with it, extend it, or contradict it?
